***The Incredible Hulk*** is a 2008 American [superhero film](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") based on the [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics") character the [Hulk](/wiki/Hulk "Hulk"). Produced by [Marvel Studios](/wiki/Marvel_Studios "Marvel Studios") and [Valhalla Motion Pictures](/wiki/Valhalla_Motion_Pictures "Valhalla Motion Pictures"), and distributed by [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"),[[a]](#cite_note-Distributor-3) it is [the second film](/wiki/List_of_Marvel_Cinematic_Universe_films "List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films") in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") (MCU). It was directed by [Louis Leterrier](/wiki/Louis_Leterrier "Louis Leterrier") from a screenplay by [Zak Penn](/wiki/Zak_Penn "Zak Penn"), and stars [Edward Norton](/wiki/Edward_Norton "Edward Norton") as [Bruce Banner](/wiki/Bruce_Banner_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Bruce Banner (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") alongside [Liv Tyler](/wiki/Liv_Tyler "Liv Tyler"), [Tim Roth](/wiki/Tim_Roth "Tim Roth"), [William Hurt](/wiki/William_Hurt "William Hurt"), [Tim Blake Nelson](/wiki/Tim_Blake_Nelson "Tim Blake Nelson"), [Ty Burrell](/wiki/Ty_Burrell "Ty Burrell"), and Christina Cabot. In the film, Banner becomes the monstrous Hulk as an unwitting pawn in a military scheme to reinvigorate the ["super soldier" program](/wiki/Project_Rebirth_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Project Rebirth (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") through [gamma radiation](/wiki/Gamma_radiation "Gamma radiation"). He goes on the run from the military while attempting to cure himself of the Hulk.

After the mixed reception to Universal's 2003 film *[Hulk](/wiki/Hulk_(film) "Hulk (film)")*, Marvel Studios reacquired the rights to the character, though Universal retained distribution rights. Leterrier was hired to direct and Penn wrote a script that was closer to the comics and the [1978–1982 television series](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(1978_TV_series) "The Incredible Hulk (1978 TV series)"). In April 2007, Norton was hired to portray Banner and to rewrite Penn's screenplay. His script positioned the film as a [reboot](/wiki/Reboot_(fiction) "Reboot (fiction)") of the series, distancing it from the 2003 film to give the new version its own identity. Norton was ultimately not credited for his writing. Filming took place from July to November 2007, primarily in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), with additional filming in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). Over 700 visual effects shots were created in post-production using a combination of [motion capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture") and [computer-generated imagery](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery"). Norton and Marvel Studios had various disagreements during production, including over the final edit.

*The Incredible Hulk* premiered at the [Gibson Amphitheatre](/wiki/Gibson_Amphitheatre "Gibson Amphitheatre") in [Universal City, California](/wiki/Universal_City,_California "Universal City, California"), on June 8, 2008, and was released in the United States on June 13 as part of [Phase One](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_One "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase One") of the MCU. It was deemed a moderate success, grossing over $264 million and receiving slightly better reviews than the 2003 film. A sequel was discussed but never materialized, in part due to rights issues with Universal. [Mark Ruffalo](/wiki/Mark_Ruffalo "Mark Ruffalo") took over the role of Banner for subsequent MCU appearances starting with the [crossover](/wiki/Crossover_(fiction) "Crossover (fiction)") film *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film) "The Avengers (2012 film)")* in 2012.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

At Culver University in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), [U.S. military](/wiki/U.S._military "U.S. military") general [Thaddeus Ross](/wiki/Thaddeus_Ross_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Thaddeus Ross (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") meets with Dr. [Bruce Banner](/wiki/Bruce_Banner_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Bruce Banner (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), the colleague and boyfriend of his daughter [Betty](/wiki/Betty_Ross_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Betty Ross (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), regarding an experiment that Ross claims is meant to make humans immune to [gamma radiation](/wiki/Gamma_radiation "Gamma radiation"). This is part of Ross's attempts to recreate a [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")-era ["super soldier" program](/wiki/Project_Rebirth_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Project Rebirth (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"). The experiment fails and Banner is exposed to high levels of gamma radiation, causing him to transform into the [Hulk](/wiki/Hulk "Hulk") if enraged or if his heart rate rises above 200 beats per minute. The Hulk destroys the lab and injures the general, Betty, and others outside. Banner becomes a fugitive from Ross, who wants to weaponize the Hulk.

Five years later,[[b]](#cite_note-11) Banner works at a bottling factory in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil, and has not transformed in five months. He trains in [yoga](/wiki/Yoga "Yoga") and breathing techniques while collaborating anonymously with an internet user called "Mr. Blue" in his search to cure his condition. After Banner accidentally cuts his finger, a drop of his blood falls into a bottle of soda which is eventually ingested by an elderly consumer in [Milwaukee, Wisconsin](/wiki/Milwaukee,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee, Wisconsin"), giving him gamma sickness. Using the bottle to track Banner, Ross sends a special forces team led by [Emil Blonsky](/wiki/Emil_Blonsky_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Emil Blonsky (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") to capture him. Banner transforms into the Hulk and defeats Blonsky's team, though Blonsky survives. After Ross explains how Banner became the Hulk, Blonsky agrees to be injected with a similar serum, gaining enhanced speed, strength, and agility.

Banner returns to Culver University and reunites with Betty. Her new boyfriend, Dr. [Leonard Samson](/wiki/Leonard_Samson_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Leonard Samson (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), contacts Ross who soon arrives with his forces and Blonsky. They attack Banner at the university and he transforms into the Hulk again. Blonsky, whose judgement is impaired by the serum, attacks and mocks the Hulk. The Hulk severely injures Blonsky, but the serum allows him to heal quickly. Betty is almost killed by military fire and is saved by the Hulk, who flees with Betty and eventually reverts to Banner. The pair go on the run, and Banner contacts Mr. Blue who urges them to meet him in New York City. Mr. Blue is cellular biologist Dr. [Samuel Sterns](/wiki/Samuel_Sterns_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Samuel Sterns (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), who has developed a possible antidote to Banner's condition. After a seemingly successful test, he warns Banner that the antidote may only reverse an initial transformation. Sterns reveals that he has [synthesized](/wiki/Organic_synthesis "Organic synthesis") Banner's blood samples, which Banner sent from Brazil, into a large supply, and wants to apply its "limitless potential" to medicine. Fearful of the Hulk's power falling into the wrong hands, Banner wants to destroy the supply.

Blonsky and Ross's forces take Banner into custody. As Banner and Betty are taken away in a helicopter, Blonsky stays behind and orders Sterns to inject him with Banner's blood, as he covets the Hulk's power. The experiment mutates Blonsky into [the Abomination](/wiki/Abomination_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Abomination (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), a monstrous creature with size and strength surpassing that of the Hulk. He attacks Sterns, who gets some of Banner's blood in a cut on his forehead and begins mutating as well. The Abomination rampages through [Harlem](/wiki/Harlem "Harlem"). Realizing that the Hulk is the only one who can stop the Abomination, Banner convinces Ross to release him. He jumps from Ross's helicopter and transforms after hitting the ground. The Hulk battles and defeats the Abomination by nearly strangling him to death with a chain, but spares his life upon hearing Betty's plea. The Hulk leaves the Abomination for Ross and his forces to arrest and flees New York.

A month later, Banner is in [Bella Coola, British Columbia](/wiki/Bella_Coola,_British_Columbia "Bella Coola, British Columbia"), where he practices transforming in a controlled manner. Sometime later, [Tony Stark](/wiki/Tony_Stark_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Tony Stark (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") approaches Ross at a bar and informs him that a team is being put together.[[c]](#cite_note-13)

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

* [Edward Norton](/wiki/Edward_Norton "Edward Norton") as [Bruce Banner](/wiki/Bruce_Banner_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Bruce Banner (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A [nuclear physicist](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics") and [biochemist](/wiki/Biochemist "Biochemist") at Culver University who, because of exposure to [gamma radiation](/wiki/Gamma_radiation "Gamma radiation"), transforms into an enormous green humanoid monster when enraged or agitated. [David Duchovny](/wiki/David_Duchovny "David Duchovny") was a front-runner for the film before Norton's casting,[[11]](#cite_note-StocksSoar-14) while director [Louis Leterrier](/wiki/Louis_Leterrier "Louis Leterrier")'s original choice was [Mark Ruffalo](/wiki/Mark_Ruffalo "Mark Ruffalo") who would later play Banner in future [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") (MCU) films.[[12]](#cite_note-HuffPost-15) Producer [Gale Anne Hurd](/wiki/Gale_Anne_Hurd "Gale Anne Hurd") recalled the duality of Norton's performances in the films *[Primal Fear](/wiki/Primal_Fear_(film) "Primal Fear (film)")* (1996) and *[Fight Club](/wiki/Fight_Club "Fight Club")* (1999),[[13]](#cite_note-NYCC2008Int-16) while Norton reminded producer [Kevin Feige](/wiki/Kevin_Feige "Kevin Feige") of [Bill Bixby](/wiki/Bill_Bixby "Bill Bixby"), who played Banner in the [1978–1982 television series](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(1978_TV_series) "The Incredible Hulk (1978 TV series)").[[14]](#cite_note-NortonBixby-17) Lou Ferrigno, who played the Hulk in that series, said Norton "has a similar physique [and a] similar personality" to Bixby.[[15]](#cite_note-LouCameo-18) Norton was a Hulk fan, citing the early comic books, the Bixby series, and [Bruce Jones](/wiki/Bruce_Jones_(comics) "Bruce Jones (comics)")'s run on the comics as his favorite depictions of the character.[[16]](#cite_note-SDCC2007Panel-19) He had expressed interest in the role for the previous film, *[Hulk](/wiki/Hulk_(film) "Hulk (film)")* (2003),[[17]](#cite_note-PennNorton-20) and was initially reluctant to take on the part after that film had strayed from the fugitive story that he associated with the character. He was won over by the vision of Leterrier and [Marvel Studios](/wiki/Marvel_Studios "Marvel Studios") and believed they were looking to him to guide the project. Norton subsequently rewrote the script.[[18]](#cite_note-NortonTotalFilm-21) Leterrier praised Norton for bringing gravitas to the script, and said they were both approaching superheroes as "the new [Greek gods](/wiki/Greek_pantheon "Greek pantheon")".[[19]](#cite_note-April2008Empire-22) Norton was particularly drawn to the Greek myth of [Prometheus](/wiki/Prometheus "Prometheus") for the character.[[20]](#cite_note-MCUReignOfMarvelStudios-23): 108[[21]](#cite_note-NortonNYTMagazine2019-24)
  + [Lou Ferrigno](/wiki/Lou_Ferrigno "Lou Ferrigno") voices the [Hulk](/wiki/Hulk "Hulk"). During the 2008 [New York Comic Con](/wiki/New_York_Comic_Con "New York Comic Con"), Leterrier publicly offered Ferrigno the role.[[22]](#cite_note-DarkHorizonsEditBay-25) This marks the third time Ferrigno portrayed the Hulk, having also voiced the character in the [1996–97 animated series](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(1996_TV_series) "The Incredible Hulk (1996 TV series)"). Originally, the Hulk's only line was "Betty" at the film's ending, which would have been his first word. Leterrier was aware that fans wanted him to speak normally and added "Leave me alone!" and "Hulk smash!". The latter line received cheers during a screening Leterrier attended.[[23]](#cite_note-IGNDirectorSpeaks-26) Ferrigno also has a [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") in the film as a security guard who is bribed by Banner with a pizza.[[24]](#cite_note-EditBayVisit-27)
* [Liv Tyler](/wiki/Liv_Tyler "Liv Tyler") as [Betty Ross](/wiki/Betty_Ross_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Betty Ross (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A [cellular biologist](/wiki/Cellular_biology "Cellular biology") and Bruce's former girlfriend, from whom he is separated as a result of his condition. Tyler was attracted to the love story in the script and was a fan of the Bixby series because of the "humanity and what [Banner] is going through".[[16]](#cite_note-SDCC2007Panel-19) She was called about the role while driving home and accepted the part after a day, without reading the script.[[25]](#cite_note-TylerTimes-28) Tyler and Norton spent hours discussing Bruce and Betty's life before he became the Hulk.[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29) She said filming the part was physical and fun,[[27]](#cite_note-TylerUSAToday-30) and compared her performance to "a deer caught in the headlights" because of Betty's shock at Bruce's unexpected return into her life.[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29)
* [Tim Roth](/wiki/Tim_Roth "Tim Roth") as [Emil Blonsky / Abomination](/wiki/Emil_Blonsky_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Emil Blonsky (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A Russian-born officer in the United Kingdom's [Royal Marines Commandos](/wiki/Royal_Marines_Commandos "Royal Marines Commandos") who is loaned to General Ross. Lusting for the Hulk's power, Blonsky is injected with various serums that transform him into a near-skeletal humanoid monster as strong as the Hulk himself. Roth said he took the part to please his sons, who are comic-book superhero fans, and because he found Leterrier's ideas "very dark and very interesting". Roth was a fan of the Bixby series as a teenager. He started watching the 2003 film to prepare for the part, but stopped as he did not want to compare himself to it.[[28]](#cite_note-RothHulk03-31) It was Roth who suggested Blonsky be a soldier rather than a [KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB") agent as in the comics.[[29]](#cite_note-IGNRoth-32) Leterrier was a fan of Roth's work and felt it was "great watching a normal [Cockney](/wiki/Cockney "Cockney") boy become a superhero",[[19]](#cite_note-April2008Empire-22) but Marvel and Norton were initially reluctant to cast him.[[30]](#cite_note-EmpireTrailer-33) Before he was cast in the film *[Punisher: War Zone](/wiki/Punisher:_War_Zone "Punisher: War Zone")* (2008), [Ray Stevenson](/wiki/Ray_Stevenson_(actor) "Ray Stevenson (actor)") was in discussions for the role.[[31]](#cite_note-RayStevenson-34) Roth trained with gun and military experts in preparation for the role,[[29]](#cite_note-IGNRoth-32) but did not work out due to Blonsky's aging. This made it difficult to film some stunts,[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29) including being pulled by a harness to show Blonsky's enhanced 30–40 mile per hour running speed.[[32]](#cite_note-DVDAnatomy-35) [Cyril Raffaelli](/wiki/Cyril_Raffaelli "Cyril Raffaelli") performed some of his stunts.[[13]](#cite_note-NYCC2008Int-16) Roth enjoyed the [motion capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture") for Abomination, which reminded him of [fringe theatre](/wiki/Fringe_theatre "Fringe theatre"). He worked with [Terry Notary](/wiki/Terry_Notary "Terry Notary"), his trainer from the film *[Planet of the Apes](/wiki/Planet_of_the_Apes_(2001_film) "Planet of the Apes (2001 film)")* (2001), on the monster's movement.[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29)[[20]](#cite_note-MCUReignOfMarvelStudios-23): 103 Roth was signed on for three more films.[[33]](#cite_note-RothSequels-36)
* [William Hurt](/wiki/William_Hurt "William Hurt") as [Thaddeus Ross](/wiki/Thaddeus_Ross_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Thaddeus Ross (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  Betty's father and a [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") [general](/wiki/General_(United_States) "General (United States)") who has dedicated his life to capturing the Hulk. Leterrier cast Hurt because "Ross is more physical, more explosive in this movie, and no actor goes from zero to 100 as well as William".[[19]](#cite_note-April2008Empire-22) He compared Ross to [Captain Ahab](/wiki/Ahab_(Moby-Dick) "Ahab (Moby-Dick)").[[30]](#cite_note-EmpireTrailer-33) The Hulk was Hurt's favorite superhero, and his son is also a big fan of the character. Hurt found production very different from the typical "pure anxiety" of a studio film, finding it more akin to an independent film.[[34]](#cite_note-HurtMTV-37) He described Ross as "humiliated by Hulk's conscience: he actually sees and recognizes that it's more developed than his own, even though he's a patriot and a warrior for his country. He's sacrificed [much] for that purpose, but at the expense at times of his humanity—which he occasionally recovers."[[35]](#cite_note-HurtCalgary-38) In June 2015, when reflecting on how his reprisal in the MCU film *[Captain America: Civil War](/wiki/Captain_America:_Civil_War "Captain America: Civil War")* (2016) was different from this film, Hurt said, "What I created [for *The Incredible Hulk*] was a Ross who was right out of the graphic novel type of thing, where he was as much of a cartoon, in a way, as the monsters were. His ego was just as big and his problems were just as big. I really did do that consciously. I created a General Ross before which created a verisimilitude for the monsters, by making him a human monster. I worked really hard on the makeup and the exaggerated behavior and things like that and a controlled psychosis."[[36]](#cite_note-HurtLATimesJune2015-39) [Sam Elliott](/wiki/Sam_Elliott "Sam Elliott") had expressed interest in reprising the role from the 2003 film before Hurt was cast.[[37]](#cite_note-ElliottMTV-40)
* [Tim Blake Nelson](/wiki/Tim_Blake_Nelson "Tim Blake Nelson") as [Samuel Sterns](/wiki/Samuel_Sterns_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Samuel Sterns (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A cellular biologist who develops a possible antidote to Banner's condition. Towards the end of the film, Sterns is exposed to a substance that begins his transformation into the comic book villain "The Leader".[[38]](#cite_note-MTVHulk2-41)
* [Ty Burrell](/wiki/Ty_Burrell "Ty Burrell") as [Leonard Samson](/wiki/Leonard_Samson_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Leonard Samson (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A psychiatrist who is in a relationship with Betty during Bruce's absence. Burrell had performed with Norton in the [off Broadway](/wiki/Off_Broadway "Off Broadway") play *[Burn This](/wiki/Burn_This "Burn This")* in 2003, and when Leterrier met him, he recognized Burrell as the "jerk" from the 2004 *[Dawn of the Dead](/wiki/Dawn_of_the_Dead_(2004_film) "Dawn of the Dead (2004 film)")* remake,[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42) which was how Samson was characterized in the script before Norton rewrote it.[[40]](#cite_note-Collider70Mins-43)
* Christina Cabot as Kathleen Sparr: A U.S. Army [major](/wiki/Major_(United_States) "Major (United States)") who is General Ross's personal aide[[41]](#cite_note-ChristinaCabot-44)

[Robert Downey Jr.](/wiki/Robert_Downey_Jr. "Robert Downey Jr.") makes an uncredited cameo appearance as [Tony Stark](/wiki/Tony_Stark_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Tony Stark (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") at the end of the film, reprising his role from the MCU film *[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(2008_film) "Iron Man (2008 film)")* (2008). Downey appeared as a favor to Marvel Studios and was frustrated that he was constantly being asked about his short appearance in the film while promoting *Iron Man*. He acknowledged that this was a smart move on Marvel's part, bringing more attention to *The Incredible Hulk*.[[42]](#cite_note-DowneyCameo-45) Hulk co-creator [Stan Lee](/wiki/Stan_Lee "Stan Lee") cameos as a man who becomes ill when drinking soda that is poisoned with Banner's blood.[[43]](#cite_note-LeeCameo-46) [Paul Soles](/wiki/Paul_Soles "Paul Soles"), who voiced Banner in the 1966 *[The Marvel Super Heroes](/wiki/The_Marvel_Super_Heroes "The Marvel Super Heroes")* animated series, appears as kindly pizzeria owner Stanley Lieber who was friends with Banner and Betty in the past. His name is a reference to Stan Lee's full name.[[44]](#cite_note-Soles-47) [Jim Wilson](/wiki/Jim_Wilson_(comics) "Jim Wilson (comics)") and [Jack McGee](/wiki/Jack_McGee_(character) "Jack McGee (character)"), supporting characters from the Hulk comics, appear as Culver University students played by P. J. Kerr and Nicholas Rose, respectively.[[45]](#cite_note-WilsonMcGee-48) Other small roles include: [Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu "Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu") artist [Rickson Gracie](/wiki/Rickson_Gracie "Rickson Gracie") as Bruce Banner's martial arts instructor, credited as an [Aikido](/wiki/Aikido "Aikido") instructor due to Norton's background with that style;[[46]](#cite_note-Gracie-49) Brazilian actress [Débora Nascimento](/wiki/D%C3%A9bora_Nascimento "Débora Nascimento") as Martina, Banner's colleague at a beverage factory;[[47]](#cite_note-SRSmallRoles-50) [Michael K. Williams](/wiki/Michael_K._Williams "Michael K. Williams") as a Harlem bystander, a role that was written for him by Norton as a fan of Williams's series *[The Wire](/wiki/The_Wire_(TV_series) "The Wire (TV series)")* (2002–2008);[[48]](#cite_note-MichaelKWilliams-51) and [Peter Mensah](/wiki/Peter_Mensah "Peter Mensah") as General Joe Greller, one of Ross's military friends.[[49]](#cite_note-SRMissingCharacters-52) Additionally, [Martin Starr](/wiki/Martin_Starr "Martin Starr") plays a college student who is credited as "Computer Nerd". In May 2019, Feige said this character had been [retroactively](/wiki/Retroactive_continuity "Retroactive continuity") named [Roger Harrington](/wiki/Roger_Harrington_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Roger Harrington (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), which is Starr's role in the MCU films *[Spider-Man: Homecoming](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Homecoming "Spider-Man: Homecoming")* (2017), *[Spider-Man: Far From Home](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Far_From_Home "Spider-Man: Far From Home")* (2019), and *[Spider-Man: No Way Home](/wiki/Spider-Man:_No_Way_Home "Spider-Man: No Way Home")* (2021).[[50]](#cite_note-FeigeStarr-53)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/0/09/The_Incredible_Hulk_film_and_TV_series_scene_comparison.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Incredible_Hulk_film_and_TV_series_scene_comparison.jpg)

The writers of the film were influenced by the TV series of the same name. Example shown above: Norton (top) sitting in a more modern version of the machine Bill Bixby (below) sat in.

After the release of [Ang Lee](/wiki/Ang_Lee "Ang Lee")'s *[Hulk](/wiki/Hulk_(film) "Hulk (film)")*, screenwriter [James Schamus](/wiki/James_Schamus "James Schamus") was planning a sequel which would continue the story featuring the Grey Hulk. He was also considering the [Leader](/wiki/Leader_(character) "Leader (character)") and the Abomination as villains.[[51]](#cite_note-AngHulkSequel-54) Marvel wanted the Abomination because he would be an actual threat to the Hulk, unlike General Ross.[[52]](#cite_note-DVDCreatingAbomb-55) During the filming of *Hulk*, producer Avi Arad had a target May 2005 theatrical release date.[[53]](#cite_note-AradMay2005-56) On January 18, 2006, Arad confirmed Marvel Studios would be providing the money for *The Incredible Hulk*'s production budget, with Universal distributing,[[54]](#cite_note-AICNHulk2-57) because Universal did not meet the deadline for filming a sequel.[[11]](#cite_note-StocksSoar-14) Marvel felt it would be better to deviate from Ang Lee's style to continue the franchise, arguing his film was like a [parallel universe](/wiki/Parallel_universe_(fiction) "Parallel universe (fiction)") [one-shot](/wiki/One-shot_(comics) "One-shot (comics)") comic book, and their next film needed to be, in Kevin Feige's words, "*really* starting the Marvel Hulk franchise". Producer Gale Anne Hurd also felt the film had to meet what "everyone expects to see from having read the comics and seen the TV series".[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29)

### Pre-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pre-production")]

Louis Leterrier, who enjoyed the TV series as a child and liked the first film,[[13]](#cite_note-NYCC2008Int-16)[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29) had expressed interest in directing the *[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(2008_film) "Iron Man (2008 film)")* film adaptation. [Jon Favreau](/wiki/Jon_Favreau "Jon Favreau") had taken that project, so Marvel offered him the Hulk. Leterrier was reluctant as he was unsure if he could replicate Lee's style, but Marvel explained that was not their intent.[[55]](#cite_note-Keown-58) Leterrier's primary inspiration was [Jeph Loeb](/wiki/Jeph_Loeb "Jeph Loeb") and [Tim Sale](/wiki/Tim_Sale_(artist) "Tim Sale (artist)")'s *[Hulk: Gray](/wiki/Hulk:_Gray "Hulk: Gray")* (a retelling of the character's first appearance). He replicated every comic book panel that he pinned-up during pre-production, from the many comics he browsed, in the final film.[[13]](#cite_note-NYCC2008Int-16) Leterrier said that he planned to show Bruce Banner's struggle with the monster within him,[[56]](#cite_note-IGNJuly2006-59) while Feige added the film would explore "that element of wish fulfillment, of overcoming an injustice or a bully and tapping into a strength that you didn't quite realize you had in yourself".[[57]](#cite_note-FeigeWish-60) Arad also said the film would be "a lot more of a love story between Bruce Banner and Betty Ross".[[58]](#cite_note-May2006Empire-61) In May 2006, Arad left Marvel Studios to become an independent producer.[[59]](#cite_note-AradLeave-62) Because he was on staff when the deal was made for *The Incredible Hulk*, he retained producer credit on the film.[[20]](#cite_note-MCUReignOfMarvelStudios-23): 59

[Zak Penn](/wiki/Zak_Penn "Zak Penn"), who wrote a draft of the first film in 1996, said the film would follow up *Hulk*, but stressed it would be more tonally similar to the TV show and [Bruce Jones](/wiki/Bruce_Jones_(comics) "Bruce Jones (comics)")' [run on the comic](/wiki/Hulk#Series_history "Hulk"). He compared his script to *[Aliens](/wiki/Aliens_(film) "Aliens (film)")*, which was a very different film from *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(film) "Alien (film)")*, but still in the same continuity.[[17]](#cite_note-PennNorton-20) He included two scenes from his 1996 script: Banner jumping from a helicopter to trigger a transformation, and realizing he is unable to have sex with Betty.[[60]](#cite_note-ThompsonScriptCredits-63) After the studio rejected a treatment by another screenwriter in 2006, Penn wrote three drafts before departing in early 2007 to promote his film *[The Grand](/wiki/The_Grand_(film) "The Grand (film)")*.[[61]](#cite_note-LATimesSigningOn-64) [Edward Norton](/wiki/Edward_Norton "Edward Norton") began discussions to play Banner in April 2006, and arranged a deal that included him as both an actor and a writer, with a screenplay draft he was contractually obligated to turn in within a month. He did so, and continued to polish his draft as late as halfway through principal photography.[[61]](#cite_note-LATimesSigningOn-64) In November 2006, a June 13, 2008, release date was set.[[62]](#cite_note-ReleaseDate-65) Leterrier acknowledged the only remaining similarity between the two films was Bruce Banner hiding in South America,[[13]](#cite_note-NYCC2008Int-16) and that the film was a unique reboot, as generally audiences would have expected another forty-minute [origin story](/wiki/Origin_story "Origin story").[[63]](#cite_note-SampleScreening-66) There were previous discussions to set the first act in Thailand.[[64]](#cite_note-TimesSquare-67) Leterrier felt audiences were left restless waiting for the character to arrive in Ang Lee's film.[[63]](#cite_note-SampleScreening-66) Feige commented, "we didn't want to tell the origin story again, because we thought people were so familiar with it, which is why we didn't tell that... One reason we made *Incredible Hulk* was to get Hulk into the [[Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe")] canon."[[65]](#cite_note-FeigeIGNApr2014-68) The end of the film occurs at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), and Leterrier was interested in naming it Empire State University, as a reference to [Peter Parker](/wiki/Peter_Parker "Peter Parker"), but was unable to since [Sony Pictures](/wiki/Sony_Pictures "Sony Pictures") controls the film rights of Spider-Man.[[66]](#cite_note-LeterrierMTVNewsJune2008-69)

Shortly after the release of *The Incredible Hulk*, Gale Anne Hurd commented on the uncertainty of its relationship with Ang Lee's *Hulk* film. "We couldn't quite figure out how to term this ... It's kind of a reboot and it's kind of sequel." Hurd said that "requel", a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau_word "Portmanteau word") of "reboot" and "sequel", was a "perfect" description for the film.[[67]](#cite_note-ColliderHurdInt-70) Feige clarified that the two films are not connected, noting how events shown in the opening credits sequence of *The Incredible Hulk* contradict *Hulk*.[[65]](#cite_note-FeigeIGNApr2014-68) Norton explained his decision to ignore Lee's origin story: "I don't even like the phrase 'origin story', and I don't think in great literature and great films that explaining the roots of the story doesn't mean it comes in the beginning."[[16]](#cite_note-SDCC2007Panel-19) "Audiences know this story," he added, "[so] deal with it artfully." He wanted to "have revelations even in the third act about what set this whole thing in motion".[[18]](#cite_note-NortonTotalFilm-21) The new origin story references [Ultimate Marvel](/wiki/Ultimate_Marvel "Ultimate Marvel")'s take on the Hulk, which also had him created in an attempt to make super soldiers.[[68]](#cite_note-ScifiWeekly-71) Norton removed [Rick Jones](/wiki/Rick_Jones_(character) "Rick Jones (character)") and toned down [S.H.I.E.L.D.](/wiki/S.H.I.E.L.D._(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "S.H.I.E.L.D. (Marvel Cinematic Universe)")'s presence.[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29) He also added the scene where Banner attempts to extract a cure from a flower and his e-mailing with Samuel Sterns,[[60]](#cite_note-ThompsonScriptCredits-63) which references Bruce Jones' story.[[69]](#cite_note-ColliderFeige-72) Norton rewrote scenes every day.[[28]](#cite_note-RothHulk03-31) Ultimately, the [Writers Guild of America](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America "Writers Guild of America") decided to credit the script solely to Penn,[[70]](#cite_note-PennSoleCredit-73) who argued Norton had not dramatically changed his script. Journalist [Anne Thompson](/wiki/Anne_Thompson_(film_critic) "Anne Thompson (film critic)") explained "The Guild tends to favor plot, structure and pre-existing characters over dialogue."[[60]](#cite_note-ThompsonScriptCredits-63) Penn said in 2008, "I wasn't happy with [Norton] coming to Comic-Con saying that he wrote the script."[[71]](#cite_note-PennUnhappy-74) Before either Penn or Norton joined the project, an anonymous screenwriter wrote a draft and lobbied for credit.[[60]](#cite_note-ThompsonScriptCredits-63)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

Leterrier had to direct four [units](/wiki/Second_unit "Second unit") with a broken foot.[[19]](#cite_note-April2008Empire-22) Filming began on July 9, 2007. Shooting primarily took place in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), because mayor [David Miller](/wiki/David_Miller_(Canadian_politician) "David Miller (Canadian politician)") is a Hulk fan and promised to be helpful to the crew when closing [Yonge Street](/wiki/Yonge_Street "Yonge Street") for four nights in September to shoot Hulk and Blonsky's climactic fight. Despite messing up the street with explosives and overturned burning vehicles, the crew would clean it up within twenty minutes so business could continue as normal each day.[[72]](#cite_note-DVDMaking-75) The first action sequence shot was the Culver University battle, which was filmed at the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto") and [Morningside Park](/wiki/Morningside_Park_(Toronto) "Morningside Park (Toronto)"). The filmmakers built a glass wall over a walkway at the university for when the soldiers trap Banner inside to smoke him out.[[32]](#cite_note-DVDAnatomy-35) There was also shooting in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Toronto "Financial District, Toronto").[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42) A factory in [Hamilton, Ontario](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), which was due for demolition, was the interior of the Brazilian factory. The site's underground floors were used for Ross's military command center.[[72]](#cite_note-DVDMaking-75) The crew also shot part of the Hulk and Blonsky's fight on a [backlot](/wiki/Backlot "Backlot") in Hamilton.[[73]](#cite_note-BRDPicture-76) Other Canadian locations included military base [CFB Trenton](/wiki/CFB_Trenton "CFB Trenton") and a glacier in [Bella Coola, British Columbia](/wiki/Bella_Coola,_British_Columbia "Bella Coola, British Columbia").[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42) Afterwards, there was a week-long shoot in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and two weeks in Rio de Janeiro.[[74]](#cite_note-FilmingCFB-77) While there, the crew shot at [Rocinha](/wiki/Rocinha "Rocinha"), [Lapa](/wiki/Lapa,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Lapa, Rio de Janeiro"), [Tijuca Forest](/wiki/Tijuca_National_Park "Tijuca National Park"), and [Santa Teresa](/wiki/Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Santa Teresa, Rio de Janeiro"). Filming concluded in November after eighty-eight days of filming.[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42)

*The Incredible Hulk* joined Toronto's Green-Screen initiative, to help cut [carbon emissions](/wiki/Carbon_emissions "Carbon emissions") and waste created during filming.[[75]](#cite_note-TorontoStar-78) Producer Gale Anne Hurd acknowledged the Hulk, being green, was a popular environmental analogy, and Norton himself is an environmentalist. Hybrid and fuel-efficient vehicles were used, with low-sulfur diesel as their energy source. The construction department used a sustainably harvested, locally sourced [yellow pine](/wiki/Pinus_echinata "Pinus echinata") instead of [lauan](/wiki/Decorative_plywood "Decorative plywood") for the sets, and also used zero- or low-[VOC](/wiki/Volatile_organic_compound "Volatile organic compound") paint. The wood was generally recycled or given to environmental organizations, and paint cans were handed to waste management. In addition, they used cloth bags, biodegradable food containers, china and silverware food utensils, a stainless steel mug for each production crew member, a contractor who removed bins, recycled paper, biodegradable soap and cleaners in the trailers and production offices, and the sound department used rechargeable batteries.[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42) *The Incredible Hulk* became the first blockbuster film to receive the [Environmental Media Association](/wiki/Environmental_Media_Association "Environmental Media Association")'s Green Seal, which is displayed during the end credits.[[76]](#cite_note-EMAGreenSeal-79)

### Post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Post-production")]

#### Editing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Editing")]

Seventy minutes of footage, mostly dealing with the origin, were not included in the final cut.[[40]](#cite_note-Collider70Mins-43) Much of this back-story was unscripted and the filmmakers were never sure of including it in the final cut, and had considered releasing some of these clips on the internet.[[24]](#cite_note-EditBayVisit-27) Editor [Kyle Cooper](/wiki/Kyle_Cooper "Kyle Cooper"), creator of the Marvel logo (with the flipping pages) and the montage detailing [Iron Man](/wiki/Tony_Stark_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Tony Stark (Marvel Cinematic Universe)")'s biography in that film, edited together much of this footage into the opening credits.[[24]](#cite_note-EditBayVisit-27) Leterrier explained a negative test screening, where flashbacks were placed across the film that the audience found too similar to *Hulk*, had resulted in compressing these to the film's start.[[40]](#cite_note-Collider70Mins-43) This replaced the original opening, where Banner comes to the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") to commit [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide"). When the scene ends, in an instant the frozen body of [Captain America](/wiki/Steve_Rogers_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Steve Rogers (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") is partially seen in the ice. Leterrier said he did not want this scene to be lost amid the opening montage.[[40]](#cite_note-Collider70Mins-43) Associate producer [Stephen Broussard](/wiki/Stephen_Broussard "Stephen Broussard") opined that the scene really worked, but given the test audience's dislike for such an opening, the filmmakers decided to move on from the planned opening and instead open the film with Bruce Banner living in Brazil after a recap telling his origin story.[[77]](#cite_note-StoryOfMarvelStudios-80)

Norton and Leterrier disputed with the producers over the final running time: they wanted it to be near 135 minutes, while the producers wanted the film to be under two hours. This was made public, and rumors spread that Norton "made it clear he won't cooperate with publicity plans if he's not happy with the final product".[[4]](#cite_note-NYTimesRuntime-5) Norton dismissed this: "Our healthy process [of collaboration], which is and should be a private matter, was misrepresented publicly as a 'dispute', seized on by people looking for a good story, and has been distorted to such a degree that it risks distracting from the film itself, which Marvel, Universal and I refuse to let happen. It has always been my firm conviction that films should speak for themselves and that knowing too much about how they are made diminishes the magic of watching them."[[78]](#cite_note-EWbtsDrama-81)

#### Visual effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Visual effects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d4/Abomination_in_The_Incredible_Hulk_%28film%29.jpg/250px-Abomination_in_The_Incredible_Hulk_%28film%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abomination_in_The_Incredible_Hulk_(film).jpg)

Leterrier's finished redesign of the Abomination

Leterrier cited [Andy Serkis](/wiki/Andy_Serkis "Andy Serkis")'s [motion capture](/wiki/Motion_capture "Motion capture") portrayals of [Gollum](/wiki/Gollum "Gollum") and [King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong "King Kong") in *[The Lord of the Rings](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings_(film_series) "The Lord of the Rings (film series)")* and *[King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong_(2005_film) "King Kong (2005 film)")*, respectively, as the standard he was aiming for with the Hulk and Abomination.[[55]](#cite_note-Keown-58) Norton and Roth filmed 2500 takes of different movements the monsters would make, such as the Hulk's "thunder claps".[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42) [Phosphorescent](/wiki/Phosphorescent "Phosphorescent") [face paint](/wiki/Face_paint "Face paint") applied to the actors' faces and [strobe lighting](/wiki/Strobe_light "Strobe light") would help record the most subtle mannerisms into the computer.[[79]](#cite_note-LATimesShade-82) Others, including [Cyril Raffaelli](/wiki/Cyril_Raffaelli "Cyril Raffaelli"), provided motion capture for stunts and fights,[[29]](#cite_note-IGNRoth-32) after the main actors had done video referencing.[[80]](#cite_note-DVDBecomingHulk-83) Leterrier hired [Rhythm & Hues Studios](/wiki/Rhythm_%26_Hues_Studios "Rhythm & Hues Studios") to provide the [computer-generated imagery](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") (CGI), rather than [Industrial Light & Magic](/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic "Industrial Light & Magic") (ILM) who created the visual effects for Ang Lee's *Hulk*. Visual effects company [Image Engine](/wiki/Image_Engine "Image Engine") spent over a year working on a shot where Banner's gamma-irradiated blood falls through three factory floors into a bottle.[[81]](#cite_note-VancouverSun-84) 700 effects shots were created for the film. Motion capture aided in placing and timing of movements, but [key frame](/wiki/Key_frame "Key frame") animation by Rhythm and Hues provided the necessary "finesse [and] superhero quality".[[82]](#cite_note-SFXfinesse-85) Many of the animators and Leterrier himself provided video reference for the climactic fight.[[32]](#cite_note-DVDAnatomy-35)

[Dale Keown](/wiki/Dale_Keown "Dale Keown")'s comic book artwork of the Hulk was an inspiration for his design.[[55]](#cite_note-Keown-58) Leterrier felt the first Hulk had "too much fat [and] the proportions were a little off". He explained, "The Hulk is beyond perfect so there is zero grams of fat, all chiseled, and his muscle and strength defines this creature so he's like a tank."[[55]](#cite_note-Keown-58) [Visual effects supervisor](/wiki/Visual_effects_supervisor "Visual effects supervisor") Kurt Williams envisioned the Hulk's physique as a [linebacker](/wiki/Linebacker "Linebacker") rather than a [bodybuilder](/wiki/Bodybuilder "Bodybuilder"). A height of nine feet was chosen for the character as they did not want him to be too inhuman. To make him more expressive, computer programs controlling the inflation of his muscles and saturation of skin color were created. Williams cited [flushing](/wiki/Flushing_(physiology) "Flushing (physiology)") as an example of humans' skin color being influenced by their emotions.[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42) The animators felt green blood would make his skin become darker rather than lighter, and his skin tones, depending on lighting, either resemble an [olive](/wiki/Olive "Olive") or even gray [slate](/wiki/Slate "Slate").[[79]](#cite_note-LATimesShade-82) His animation model was completed without the effects company's full knowledge of what he would be required to do: he was rigged to do whatever they imagined, in case the model was to be used for *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film) "The Avengers (2012 film)")* film.[[82]](#cite_note-SFXfinesse-85) The Hulk's medium-length hair was modeled on [Mike Deodato](/wiki/Mike_Deodato "Mike Deodato")'s art.[[82]](#cite_note-SFXfinesse-85) He originally had a [crew cut](/wiki/Crew_cut "Crew cut"), but Leterrier decided flopping hair imbued him with more character.[[80]](#cite_note-DVDBecomingHulk-83) Leterrier cited *[An American Werewolf in London](/wiki/An_American_Werewolf_in_London "An American Werewolf in London")* as the inspiration for Banner's transformation, wanting to show how painful it was for him to change.[[83]](#cite_note-AICNEditingBay-86) As a nod to the live-action TV series, Banner's eyes change color first when he transforms.[[84]](#cite_note-MTVEyes-87) Leterrier changed the Abomination's design from the comics because he felt the audience would question why he resembled a fish or a reptile, instead of "an [über](/wiki/%C3%9Cber "Über")-human" like the Hulk. Rather, his hideousness is derived from being injected multiple times into his skin, muscles, and bones, creating a creature with a protruding spine and sharp bones that he can use to stab. His green skin is pale and reflects light, so it appears orange because of the surrounding fire during the climactic battle.[[30]](#cite_note-EmpireTrailer-33) The motion capture performers, including Roth, tried to make the character behave less gracefully than the Hulk. They modeled his posture and the way he turns his head on a shark.[[52]](#cite_note-DVDCreatingAbomb-55) The character also shares Roth's tattoos.[[85]](#cite_note-RothMTV-88) A height of eleven feet was chosen for the character.[[39]](#cite_note-ProdNotes-42) Leterrier tried to work in the character's pointed ears, but realized the Hulk would bite them off (using the [example](/wiki/Evander_Holyfield_vs._Mike_Tyson_II#Match "Evander Holyfield vs. Mike Tyson II") of [Mike Tyson](/wiki/Mike_Tyson "Mike Tyson") when he fought [Evander Holyfield](/wiki/Evander_Holyfield "Evander Holyfield")), and felt ignoring that would make the Hulk come across as stupid.[[86]](#cite_note-AbombEars-89)

Leterrier had planned to use [prosthetic makeup](/wiki/Prosthetic_makeup "Prosthetic makeup") and [animatronics](/wiki/Animatronic "Animatronic") to complement the [computer-generated imagery](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") that was solely used in the previous film.[[87]](#cite_note-Animatronic-90) The make-up artists who worked on *[X-Men: The Last Stand](/wiki/X-Men:_The_Last_Stand "X-Men: The Last Stand")* were set to portray Blonsky's gradual transformation,[[28]](#cite_note-RothHulk03-31) which Zak Penn said would portray Blonsky "not [being] used to having these properties. Like he's much heavier, and we talked about how when he walks down the sidewalk, his weight destroys the sidewalk and he's tripping. [It's all about] the humanization of these kinds of superhero characters, showing the effects physics may actually have on [them]."[[88]](#cite_note-PennBlonsky-91) Tom Woodruff Jr. of Amalgamated Dynamics (who created all the costumes for the *[Alien](/wiki/Alien_(franchise) "Alien (franchise)")* films since *[Alien 3](/wiki/Alien_3 "Alien 3")*) was in negotiations and created two busts of the Hulk and prosthetic hands to act as stand-ins for the character. A full animatronic was never created as it was decided it would complicate production to set up shots for a puppet and then a computer graphic.[[89]](#cite_note-Woodruff-92) An animatronic was used for Sterns' mutating head, however.[[73]](#cite_note-BRDPicture-76) Destruction was mostly done practically. A model of a bottling machine was smashed through a wall for when the Hulk escapes the factory. The filmmakers used steam and [dry ice](/wiki/Dry_ice "Dry ice") for the gas used to smoke out the Hulk, and they destroyed a real Humvee by dropping a weight on it when shooting the Culver University battle. Pipes blew fire for when the Hulk strikes down the computer-generated helicopter. When Banner falls from the helicopter to trigger the Hulk into fighting the Abomination, Norton was attached to a surface held by a bar which turned 90 degrees while the camera was pulled to the ceiling to simulate falling. Leterrier jokingly remarked that making Norton fall that distance would render him unable to act.[[32]](#cite_note-DVDAnatomy-35)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Music")]

Further information: [The Incredible Hulk (soundtrack)](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(soundtrack) "The Incredible Hulk (soundtrack)")

The score for the film was composed by [Craig Armstrong](/wiki/Craig_Armstrong_(composer) "Craig Armstrong (composer)"), who was the [arranger](/wiki/Arrangement "Arrangement") for [Massive Attack](/wiki/Massive_Attack "Massive Attack"), a band Leterrier was fond of and had collaborated with on the 2005 film *[Unleashed](/wiki/Unleashed_(2005_film) "Unleashed (2005 film)")*. Armstrong was his first choice, which surprised Marvel, not knowing if he had scored an action film (he did compose 2001's *[Kiss of the Dragon](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Dragon "Kiss of the Dragon")*).[[90]](#cite_note-MusicEye-93) At Leterrier's suggestion, the soundtrack was released on a two-disc album, which Armstrong thought was a joke until he compiled the album and Marvel asked him why they were given only one disc.[[91]](#cite_note-MusicHidden-94) The film's score includes a reference to [Joe Harnell](/wiki/Joe_Harnell "Joe Harnell")'s theme "The Lonely Man" from the 1978 *Incredible Hulk* television series.[[92]](#cite_note-Harnell-95)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Marketing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Louis-Leterrier-Incroyable-Hulk-2.JPG/250px-Louis-Leterrier-Incroyable-Hulk-2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Louis-Leterrier-Incroyable-Hulk-2.JPG)

[Louis Leterrier](/wiki/Louis_Leterrier "Louis Leterrier") promoting the film in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") in July 2008

> "We know the Hulk from 2003 didn't satisfy the fans, and we had to acknowledge that. We emphasized the passion that fans still have for this character and that this is the movie people have always wanted."

—[Stephanie Sperber](/wiki/Stephanie_Sperber "Stephanie Sperber"), executive vice-president of Universal Studios Partnerships[[93]](#cite_note-BrandsBulk-96)

Effort was made to promote the story as having a romance and a physical antagonist, and the title was used for promotional puns (such as [7-Eleven](/wiki/7-Eleven "7-Eleven")'s "Incredible Gulp" Slurpees, and "Incredible Dad" themed [Father's Day](/wiki/Father%27s_Day "Father's Day") gifts at [Kmart](/wiki/Kmart_(United_States) "Kmart (United States)")). [Burger King](/wiki/Burger_King "Burger King") also promoted the film, and [General Nutrition Centers](/wiki/General_Nutrition_Centers "General Nutrition Centers") used the title character as a role model for strength training.[[93]](#cite_note-BrandsBulk-96) [Hasbro](/wiki/Hasbro "Hasbro") created the toy line, which they released on May 3, 2008.[[94]](#cite_note-Toys-97) [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") released two tie-in video games on June 5, 2008; a [version for consoles and personal computers](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(2008_video_game) "The Incredible Hulk (2008 video game)") was developed by [Edge of Reality](/wiki/Edge_of_Reality "Edge of Reality"), while a [handheld version](/wiki/The_Incredible_Hulk_(Nintendo_DS_video_game) "The Incredible Hulk (Nintendo DS video game)") for the [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") was developed by [Amaze Entertainment](/wiki/Amaze_Entertainment "Amaze Entertainment").[[95]](#cite_note-GameDevelopers-98)[[96]](#cite_note-Shelves-99) The film was promoted in an episode of *[American Gladiators](/wiki/American_Gladiators_(2008_TV_series) "American Gladiators (2008 TV series)")* on June 9, 2008, which was hosted by [Hulk Hogan](/wiki/Hulk_Hogan "Hulk Hogan") and featured [Lou Ferrigno](/wiki/Lou_Ferrigno "Lou Ferrigno").[[97]](#cite_note-AmericanGladiatorsPromo-100)

Following the editing dispute between Norton and Leterrier, Universal's Adam Fogelson and Norton planned a promotional tour that would avoid constant media interviews and therefore uncomfortable questions. Norton attended the premiere, took part in a *[Jimmy Kimmel Live!](/wiki/Jimmy_Kimmel_Live! "Jimmy Kimmel Live!")* sketch, and would also promote the film in Japan.[[60]](#cite_note-ThompsonScriptCredits-63) However, during the film's release he chose to do charity work in Africa.[[98]](#cite_note-NortonAfrica-101)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Release")]

### Theatrical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Theatrical")]

*The Incredible Hulk* premiered on June 8, 2008, at the [Gibson Amphitheatre](/wiki/Gibson_Amphitheatre "Gibson Amphitheatre") in [Universal City, California](/wiki/Universal_City,_California "Universal City, California")[[99]](#cite_note-Premiere-102) and was released in theaters on June 13 in the United States,[[62]](#cite_note-ReleaseDate-65) where it opened in 3,505 theaters.[[100]](#cite_note-BOMWeekends-103) The film is part of [Phase One](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_One "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase One") of the MCU.[[101]](#cite_note-PhaseOne-104) *The Incredible Hulk* was formatted and screened in [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") for the first time on August 30, 2018, as part of Marvel Studios' 10 year anniversary IMAX festival.[[102]](#cite_note-IMAX10thAnniversaryFestival-105)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Home media")]

*The Incredible Hulk* was released by [Universal Studios Home Entertainment](/wiki/Universal_Pictures_Home_Entertainment "Universal Pictures Home Entertainment") on [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") and [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") on October 21, 2008. It includes behind-the-scenes featurettes, audio commentary, deleted scenes, and an alternate opening.[[103]](#cite_note-DVDDetails-106)[[104]](#cite_note-BRDDetails-107)

The film was also collected in a 10-disc [box set](/wiki/Box_set "Box set") titled "[Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase One – Avengers Assembled](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_One#Home_media "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase One")" which includes all of the Phase One films in the Marvel Cinematic Universe.[[105]](#cite_note-BriefcaseSet-108) It was released by [Walt Disney Studios Home Entertainment](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_Home_Entertainment "Walt Disney Studios Home Entertainment") on April 2, 2013.[[106]](#cite_note-BriefcaseDelayed-109) Universal released the film on [Ultra HD Blu-ray](/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray") on April 10, 2018.[[107]](#cite_note-UltraHD-110) *The Incredible Hulk* began streaming on [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") in the United States on June 16, 2023, after the distribution rights to the film reverted to Marvel Studios and Disney from Universal.[[1]](#cite_note-Disney+-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Disney+2-2)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Box office")]

*The Incredible Hulk* grossed $134.8 million in the United States and Canada, and $130 million in other territories, for a worldwide total of $264.8 million.[[6]](#cite_note-BOM-7) The film, which cost more than $200 million including a production budget of $150 million and marketing costs,[[5]](#cite_note-LetterierPost-6)[[108]](#cite_note-THRBO-111) barely passed the box office of its predecessor which cost less and was deemed a commercial failure. However, *The Incredible Hulk* was considered to be a success, albeit on a smaller scale than *Iron Man*.[[108]](#cite_note-THRBO-111)[[109]](#cite_note-GuardianSuccess-112) Marvel and Universal both expressed happiness with the film's financial performance. Entertainment analyst David Davis explained that the first film was seen as a commercial failure due to its high expectations while Marvel was able to downplay expectations for this film, especially after the success of *Iron Man*. Davis said *The Incredible Hulk* "overdelivered, relative to its underpromise".[[108]](#cite_note-THRBO-111)

*The Incredible Hulk* earned $55.4 million in its opening weekend, becoming the top film at the box office.[[100]](#cite_note-BOMWeekends-103) Behind *[Fantastic Four: Rise of the Silver Surfer](/wiki/Fantastic_Four:_Rise_of_the_Silver_Surfer "Fantastic Four: Rise of the Silver Surfer")*, it was the second-highest gross for a film released over the [Father's Day](/wiki/Father%27s_Day "Father's Day") weekend.[[110]](#cite_note-VarietyBO-113) This surpassed industry expectations of a $45 million opening, following the disappointing response to the 2003 film. Universal believed [word of mouth](/wiki/Word_of_mouth "Word of mouth") would contribute to the film breaking even eventually.[[111]](#cite_note-LATimesBO-114)

*The Incredible Hulk* also opened in 38 other countries, adding $31 million to the total opening. The film outgrossed the 2003 film in South Korea, while its openings in Mexico and Russia created records for Universal.[[112]](#cite_note-VarietyOverseasBO-115) The film grossed 24 million [yuan](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") (roughly $3.4 million) in its Chinese opening on August 26, outgrossing the previous film's overall gross of 10 million yuan.[[113]](#cite_note-ChineseBO-116)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On review aggregator [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), 68% of 238 critics gave *The Incredible Hulk* a positive review, with an average score of 6.4/10. The critics consensus reads, "*The Incredible Hulk* may not be quite the smashing success that fans of Marvel's raging behemoth might hope for, but it offers more than enough big green action to make up for its occasionally puny narrative."[[114]](#cite_note-117) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_average "Weighted average"), assigned the film a score of 61 out of 100 based on 38 critics, indicating "generally favorable" reviews.[[115]](#cite_note-118) The critical response was described as an improvement over that of the 2003 film,[[116]](#cite_note-Time50Years-119) although the Rotten Tomatoes score was only a little higher for *The Incredible Hulk*.[[117]](#cite_note-VarietyBOMyths-120) *[Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters")* reported that this film received "slightly better reviews" than the previous film.[[118]](#cite_note-ReutersBO-121) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A−" on an A+ to F scale.[[119]](#cite_note-CinemaScore-122)

Todd McCarthy of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* said, "what seemed, in theory, the least-necessary revival of a big screen superhero emerges as perfectly solid summer action fare in *The Incredible Hulk*." He emphasized "it's all par-for-the-course cinematic demolition and destruction, staged efficiently and with a hint of enthusiasm," and "penned with sporadic wit [...] Visuals lean toward the dark and murky, but editing by three—actually six—hands is fleet, and Craig Armstrong's ever-present score is simultaneously bombastic and helpfully supportive of the action. Effects are in line with pic's generally pro but not inspired achievements."[[120]](#cite_note-McCarthyReview-123) Rene Rodriguez of *[The Miami Herald](/wiki/The_Miami_Herald "The Miami Herald")* applauded that the film "does a lot of things [Ang] Lee's *Hulk* didn't: it's lighter and faster-paced, it's funnier and it embraces (instead of ignoring) the 1970s TV series that furthered the character's popularity".[[121]](#cite_note-RodriguezReview-124) Mark Rahner of *[The Seattle Times](/wiki/The_Seattle_Times "The Seattle Times")* wrote that, "The relaunch of Marvel's green goliath is an improvement over director Ang Lee's ponderous 2003 *Hulk* in nearly every way – except that the actual Hulk still looks scarcely better than something from a video game, and he still barely talks".[[122]](#cite_note-RahnerReview-125) [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") was not a fan of the film stating, "*The Incredible Hulk* is no doubt an ideal version of the Hulk saga for those who found Ang Lee's *Hulk* too talky, or dare I say, too thoughtful. But not for me. It sidesteps the intriguing aspects of Hulkdom and spends way too much time in, dare I say, noisy and mindless action sequences."[[123]](#cite_note-EbertReview-126)

[Christy Lemire](/wiki/Christy_Lemire "Christy Lemire") of the *[Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press")* found that "the inevitable comparisons to *Iron Man*, Marvel Studios' first blockbuster this summer, serve as a glaring reminder of what this Hulk lacks: wit and heart. Despite the presence of Edward Norton, an actor capable of going just as deep as Robert Downey Jr., we don't feel a strong sense of Bruce Banner's inner conflict".[[124]](#cite_note-LemireReview-127) [A.O. Scott](/wiki/A.O._Scott "A.O. Scott") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* opined, "'The Adequate Hulk' would have been a more suitable title. There are some big, thumping fights and a few bright shards of pop-cultural wit, but for the most part this movie seems content to aim for the generic mean".[[125]](#cite_note-ScottReview-128) [David Ansen](/wiki/David_Ansen "David Ansen") of *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* wrote, "Leterrier has style, he's good with action and he's eager to give the audience its money's worth of bone-crunching battles. Still, once the movie leaves the atmospheric Brazilian settings, nothing in this "Hulk" sinks in deeply: its familiar genre pleasures are all on the surface. ... The movie's scene stealer is Tim Blake Nelson, making a comically welcome third act appearance as the unethical but madly enthusiastic scientist Samuel Sterns".[[126]](#cite_note-AnsenReview-129)

## Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Future")]

### Further MCU appearances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Further MCU appearances")]

Further information: [List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films](/wiki/List_of_Marvel_Cinematic_Universe_films "List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films"), [Hulk in other media § Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Hulk_in_other_media#Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Hulk in other media"), and [*She-Hulk: Attorney at Law*](/wiki/She-Hulk:_Attorney_at_Law "She-Hulk: Attorney at Law")

[Mark Ruffalo](/wiki/Mark_Ruffalo "Mark Ruffalo"), Leterrier's first choice for Banner,[[12]](#cite_note-HuffPost-15) replaced Norton as Banner / Hulk in the MCU beginning in *The Avengers*,[[131]](#cite_note-DeadlineRuffalo-134) after Feige said he chose not to bring back Norton. He explained: "Our decision is definitely not one based on monetary factors, but instead rooted in the need for an actor who embodies the creativity and collaborative spirit of our other talented cast members. *The Avengers* demands players who thrive working as part of an ensemble".[[132]](#cite_note-NortonReplaced-135) Norton felt this statement was "cheap".[[21]](#cite_note-NortonNYTMagazine2019-24) In October 2014, Norton claimed he chose never to play Hulk again because he "wanted more diversity" with his career, and did not want to be associated with only one character.[[133]](#cite_note-NortonTHR-136) Ruffalo also portrayed the character in *[Avengers: Age of Ultron](/wiki/Avengers:_Age_of_Ultron "Avengers: Age of Ultron")* (2015),[[134]](#cite_note-RuffaloAoU-137) *[Thor: Ragnarok](/wiki/Thor:_Ragnarok "Thor: Ragnarok")* (2017), *[Avengers: Infinity War](/wiki/Avengers:_Infinity_War "Avengers: Infinity War")* (2018), and *[Avengers: Endgame](/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame "Avengers: Endgame")* (2019).[[135]](#cite_note-RuffaloYahooJan2016-138) Ruffalo said Banner was able to have a more prominent role in *Ragnarok*, *Infinity War*, and *Endgame* because of the lack of a standalone Hulk film, with the character's arc across those films "feel[ing] like a Hulk movie".[[135]](#cite_note-RuffaloYahooJan2016-138) Ruffalo also made appearances in [post- and mid- credits scenes](/wiki/Post-credits_scene "Post-credits scene") of *[Iron Man 3](/wiki/Iron_Man_3 "Iron Man 3")* (2013),[[136]](#cite_note-RuffaloIM3-139) *[Captain Marvel](/wiki/Captain_Marvel_(film) "Captain Marvel (film)")* (2019),[[137]](#cite_note-RuffaloCM-140) and *[Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings](/wiki/Shang-Chi_and_the_Legend_of_the_Ten_Rings "Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings")* (2021).[[138]](#cite_note-RuffaloSCLTR-141)

Hurt first reprised his role as Thaddeus Ross in *[Captain America: Civil War](/wiki/Captain_America:_Civil_War "Captain America: Civil War")* (2016).[[139]](#cite_note-HurtCivilWar-142) Ruffalo and Roth both reprised their roles in the [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") series *[She-Hulk: Attorney at Law](/wiki/She-Hulk:_Attorney_at_Law "She-Hulk: Attorney at Law")* (2022).[[140]](#cite_note-RuffaloRothSheHulk-143) Roth also provided uncredited vocals for Abomination in *Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings*.[[141]](#cite_note-AbominationShangChi-144) Nelson and Tyler reprised their respective roles as Samuel Sterns and Betty Ross in *[Captain America: Brave New World](/wiki/Captain_America:_Brave_New_World "Captain America: Brave New World")* (2025),[[142]](#cite_note-SternsBNW-145)[[143]](#cite_note-TylerBNW-146) while [Harrison Ford](/wiki/Harrison_Ford "Harrison Ford") was cast to play Thaddeus Ross in *Brave New World* in the wake of Hurt's death in 2022.[[144]](#cite_note-FordBNW-147)

### Potential sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Incredible_Hulk_(film)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Potential sequel")]

In March 2008, Norton said "a lot" was left out of the film because it had been envisioned as multiple parts, with this film "intended as chapter one".[[18]](#cite_note-NortonTotalFilm-21) Leterrier made the film's final shot of Banner ambiguous; the thought being if there was a sequel, it would mean Banner finally masters control over his anger; if there was not a sequel, the shot indicated instead that he becomes a menace in *The Avengers*.[[145]](#cite_note-MTVHulkVillainAvengers-148) Leterrier had also intended for a scene in the credits showing Blonsky, human once more, imprisoned and chained in a box,[[146]](#cite_note-DVDCommentary-149) with Feige originally having an idea that the character would be locked in a steel vault that would have been sunk to the bottom of the ocean.[[147]](#cite_note-RothOriginalAbominationPlan-150) The character of Samuel Sterns, played by [Tim Blake Nelson](/wiki/Tim_Blake_Nelson "Tim Blake Nelson"), was introduced to set him up as a villain in a possible future film, where he would become the Leader.[[26]](#cite_note-June2008Empire-29)[[148]](#cite_note-NelsonMTV-151) Aaron Sims, the lead designer on *The Incredible Hulk*, also took time to work on concepts for the Leader.[[149]](#cite_note-LeaderDesigns-152) [Ty Burrell](/wiki/Ty_Burrell "Ty Burrell") was also interested in portraying Doc Samson more faithfully to how he appears in the comics.[[38]](#cite_note-MTVHulk2-41)

Leterrier and Roth were originally contracted to return. Leterrier also stated Norton was not signed on,[[150]](#cite_note-NortonNotSigned-153) but in October, Hurd stated that Norton was contracted to reprise the role.[[151]](#cite_note-MTVHurdSequel-154) The film had outgrossed its predecessor and Universal indicated interest in a sequel,[[152]](#cite_note-VarietyOutperform-155) though Leterrier believed a sequel would not be made because of the film's box office return.[[153]](#cite_note-LeterrierDH-156) Feige said the film met Marvel's expectations and that Hulk would return, but after *The Avengers*.[[154]](#cite_note-MTVFeigeHulkAgain-157) Hurd was not concerned that a sequel may not be produced until at least 2012, citing the positive reception to the film and having produced the *[Terminator](/wiki/Terminator_(franchise) "Terminator (franchise)")* series, the second and third film of which had a 12-year gap.[[151]](#cite_note-MTVHurdSequel-154) Leterrier, after having previously said he did not want to direct a sequel,[[155]](#cite_note-LeterrierNo-158) said in late 2009 he had changed his mind and was now amenable.[[156]](#cite_note-LeterrierYes-159) In October 2010, [Marvel Television](/wiki/Marvel_Television "Marvel Television") announced the development of a Hulk television series for [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"),[[157]](#cite_note-HulkTVSeries-160) which was planned to explore more of Banner's relationship with Betty Ross at the very beginning of the Hulk's existence.[[158]](#cite_note-HulkTVFeigeIGN-161) Feige said the series could ultimately link up with the films,[[158]](#cite_note-HulkTVFeigeIGN-161)[[159]](#cite_note-HulkTVFeige/Film-162) but the project was eventually shelved by November 2013.[[160]](#cite_note-DisneyNetflixVariety-163)

In April 2012, despite Ruffalo being on board to play the Hulk in the sequel, Feige confirmed that Marvel had no plans at that time to film another Hulk film.[[161]](#cite_note-April2012NoSequel-164) In a Q&A session, Feige and Ruffalo confirmed that discussions were underway to produce another Hulk film due to the positive audience response to Ruffalo's performance in *The Avengers*.[[162]](#cite_note-SequelPossible-165) In September, Feige, while exploring all possible story options for a sequel film, including a film based on the "[Planet Hulk](/wiki/Planet_Hulk "Planet Hulk")" and "[World War Hulk](/wiki/World_War_Hulk "World War Hulk")" storylines, stated all stories from the comics were "on the table" and that the character could "carry a movie and be as entertaining" as he was in *The Avengers*. He added that Marvel Studios would not consider a sequel until after *Age of Ultron*.[[163]](#cite_note-Sep2012Sequel-166)

In June 2014, Ruffalo said he believed the studio might be considering doing a new standalone Hulk film, saying, "I think they are, for the first time, entertaining the idea of it. When we did *The Avengers* it was basically 'No!', and now there is some consideration for it. But there's still nothing definitive, not even a skeletal version of what it would be".[[164]](#cite_note-RuffaloSequelJune2014-167) In July, Feige stated that the studio was not considering a "Planet Hulk" film at that time, due to wanting to feature Ruffalo's Banner in the film. However, he did not rule out a story that saw the Hulk and Banner end up in space and explained why a solo Hulk film did not occur in [Phase Two](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Two "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Two") of the MCU by saying the studio wanted to "save" one of the original Avengers characters for just the *Avengers* films, with the others appearing in their own solo films (Iron Man, Thor, and Captain America) or the films of others (Black Widow and Nick Fury in *[Captain America: The Winter Soldier](/wiki/Captain_America:_The_Winter_Soldier "Captain America: The Winter Soldier")*).[[165]](#cite_note-FeigeIGNJuly2014-168) In October, again on a solo film, Feige said, "We'll see. We'd love to do it, we'd love to find the place to put it", but that the character would make appearances in other character's films in [Phase Three](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Three "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Three").[[166]](#cite_note-Oct2014EventQA-169) In August 2022, Ruffalo noted there had been conversations around exploring the two-year time period of Hulk arriving on Sakaar, as well as how Banner and Hulk were able to integrate to become Smart Hulk. He was also open to adapting more of *Planet Hulk* or *World War Hulk*, which he said the fans were hoping to see.[[167]](#cite_note-RuffaloVarietyAug2022-170)

In April 2015, Ruffalo said Universal holding the distribution rights to Hulk films may be an obstacle to releasing a future Hulk standalone film,[[168]](#cite_note-RuffaloApril2015-171) and later reiterated this in October,[[169]](#cite_note-RuffaloUSAToday-172) and July 2017.[[170]](#cite_note-RuffaloJuly2017-173) Marvel regained the film production rights for the character in February 2006,[[11]](#cite_note-StocksSoar-14) but Universal retained the distribution rights for *The Incredible Hulk* as well as the [right of first refusal](/wiki/Right_of_first_refusal "Right of first refusal") to pick up the distribution rights to each of any future Marvel-produced Hulk films.[[171]](#cite_note-HulkRights-174) According to *The Hollywood Reporter*, a potential reason why Marvel had not reacquired the film distribution rights to the Hulk as they did with [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") for the Iron Man, Thor, and Captain America films is that Universal holds the [theme park](/wiki/Universal_Destinations_%26_Experiences "Universal Destinations & Experiences") rights to several Marvel characters that Marvel's parent company, [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company"), wants for its own [theme parks](/wiki/Disney_Parks,_Experiences_and_Products "Disney Parks, Experiences and Products").[[172]](#cite_note-THRJulyl2015-175) In December, Ruffalo stated that the strained relationship between Marvel and Universal may be another obstacle to releasing a future standalone Hulk film.[[173]](#cite_note-RuffaloYahooDec2015-176) MCU director [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn") expressed interest in doing a film featuring both Hulk and Red Hulk, but the project never entered development due to conflicts with Universal;[[174]](#cite_note-GunnRedHulk-177) Red Hulk first appears in the MCU in *Brave New World*.[[175]](#cite_note-BNWRedHulk-178)

By August 2022, reports believed it was possible Marvel Studios would regain the distribution rights to the character from Universal in 2023.[[176]](#cite_note-HulkRightsReturn?Aug2022-179)[[177]](#cite_note-DoGHulkRights2023-180) This was due to [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") filings from Marvel Entertainment that indicated Universal had agreed to distribute *The Incredible Hulk* under the same terms Marvel agreed to with Paramount, which held distribution rights for 15 years from its first release.[[178]](#cite_note-SECFilings-181) In March 2023, [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup") [financial analyst](/wiki/Financial_analyst "Financial analyst") Jason Bazinet felt Disney may try to include the distribution rights to Hulk, as well as [Namor](/wiki/Namor "Namor"), in any potential sale of the streaming service [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") to [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"), the owner of Universal Pictures through [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal").[[179]](#cite_note-BazinetRightsHuluSale-182) The distribution rights to *The Incredible Hulk* returned to Marvel Studios and Disney by June 15, 2023.[[1]](#cite_note-Disney+-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Disney+2-2) In August 2023, Leterrier revealed a potential sequel had considered featuring Hulk's [Grey Hulk](/wiki/Grey_Hulk "Grey Hulk") persona as well as multiple Red Hulks.[[180]](#cite_note-LeterrierSequelPlansAug2023-183) In February 2024, Ruffalo expressed doubt over the likelihood of making a standalone Hulk film, citing the cost of the CGI required for the character as a reason.[[181]](#cite_note-RuffaloFeb2024-184)
